返回本批次

硅谷精英的反智主义

The Anti-Intellectualism of Silicon Valley Elites

www.elizabethspiers.com 作者 speckx 分数 76 评论 13 2026/04/02 04:21
打开 HN 原帖 打开原始文章

中文摘要

  1. 我最近重读了理查德·霍夫施塔特（Richard Hofstadter）的《美国生活中的反智主义》，现在每当一位反动的硅谷亿万富翁开口说话时，我都会想到这一点。所以我为《国家》写了这篇文章。专栏如下：在 Instagram 上，有一位名叫 Brian Patrick (@pano.dime) 的活动家，他的账户致力于“发布 2026 年人工智能高管每天所说的疯狂言论”。我无法停止思考他在第 15 天的发言，引用了一家名为 Suno 的公司的首席执行官 Mikey Shulman 的话，他声称音乐家讨厌制作音乐的过程。 “现在做音乐真的不太有趣，”他说。 “这需要大量的时间、大量的练习，你需要真正擅长一种乐器或真正擅长一款制作软件。我认为大多数人并不喜欢他们花在制作音乐上的大部分时间。”这对我认识的每一位专业音乐家来说都是新闻，我住在布鲁克林的一个地区，毗邻一个我认为是“爸爸乐队之地”的社区，因为那里住着不成比例的带孩子的老年独立摇滚乐手。但令我印象深刻的并不是舒尔曼的言论的荒谬性；而是他的言论。正如历史学家理查德·霍夫施塔特（Richard Hofstadter）指出的那样，强烈的反智精神长期以来一直活跃着美国文化，但它通常针对的是自下而上的知识精英。当今反智主义品牌的引人注目之处在于它渗透到了美国的知识精英之中。它源于科技寡头们的基本信念，即他们已经掌握了一切，没有什么可以学习的了。在这种技术驱动学习的隐世愿景中，他们认为深度智力工作（例如，当你创作一首复杂的音乐时所做的那种工作）几乎没有或根本没有内在价值。他们对人工智能的蔑视加剧了他们对高等教育、人文学科和为学习而学习的攻击，他们认为这些除了不可避免的数字化和货币化之外没有任何目的。这样的例子无处不在：彼得·泰尔(Peter Thiel)反对大学入学率的运动和他为想要放弃大学的高中生提供补贴的计划，马克·安德森(Marc Andreessen)吹嘘自己积极避免内省，泰尔的Palantir同事亚历克斯·卡普(Alex Karp)兴高采烈地预测人工智能会伤害他们。受过教育的女性最多。所有这些为学习而学习的祸害本身都是特权教育的受益者，但这并不重要：作为热心的垄断者，他们成功地相信自己已经凭借批判性思维垄断了市场。其他人不必为学习的严格性所困扰，因为他们的存在只是为了在未来的科技政权中充当无人机。这种姿态的讽刺之处在于，美国生活中几乎没有一个领域——除了特朗普白宫的科技界政治人物之外——比硅谷更不欢迎严谨的思维。算法主导地位的拥护者是聊天机器人和技术官僚思维和推理捷径的拥护者，他们自己也广泛使用它们，尽管这些模型会产生幻觉，并且有一种恶意的阿谀奉承的倾向。 《纽约时报》最近一篇关于人工智能聊天机器人爆炸式增长的报道称，“研究人员发现，近十几个领先模型都非常阿谀奉承，在人际冲突中站在用户一边的次数比人类多 49%，即使用户描述了他们违法、伤害他人或撒谎的情况。”人工智能革命中谄媚的知识礼宾也减轻了用户的认知压力，从而进一步削弱了他们的思维能力。麻省理工学院媒体实验室的一项题为“ChatGPT 上的大脑”的研究发现，LLM 用户“在神经、语言和行为层面上始终表现不佳”。科技寡头们以某种方式设法使思维变得“天真”。对于那些了解硅谷冷酷无情、幽闭思维的学生来说，这应该不会感到惊讶。科技寡头们建立了一种新的认知技术，旨在在用户通过现实世界版本的相同过程有效地对自己进行脑白质切除后，对他们的大脑进行煎炸。我们的技术领主长期以来一直将他们的思维外包给许多致力于消化困难材料并为他们总结的人（和技术）。在他们的工作生活中，他们周围都是唯唯诺诺的人和同事，他们对他们所说的一切都予以肯定。每次特朗普内阁会议上爆发的伟大领袖谄媚表现的测试版在硅谷的董事会中得到了完善。这种精心呵护的特权泡沫使科技寡头很容易避免因质疑他们的存在模式或面对哪怕是轻微的逆境而带来的任何不适。几年前的一条推文简洁地总结了这种生活方式的精神成本：“成为亿万富翁肯定是疯了。你可以买新牙、新皮肤。你所有的椅子都价值 20,000 美元，重 2,000 磅。你的生活只是一系列你自己的喜好。就认知障碍而言，这可能就像每天被马踢头一样。”霍夫施塔德勒 1964 年获得普利策奖的著作《美国生活中的反智主义》在某些方面已经过时，但它出色地将反智主义的教条追溯到了我们的建国神话，尤其是商业阶级对白手起家的人的崇拜。白手起家的人始终是一个自私自利的寓言，旨在掩盖商业贵族统治的深刻裂痕。现在，美国的大部分财富都是继承的，或者是运气和股权增值的产物，这与任何创始人或首席执行官的物质贡献极不成比例，我们的亿万富翁企业家和企业主甚至比以前更不白手起家了。尽管如此，这个神话仍然存在，你可以从科技寡头坚持认为他们不欠社会其他人任何东西作为他们自己的普罗米修斯天才的结果中看到这一点。这就是硅谷的愿景背后的逻辑，即彻底的寡头背叛与堕落的人类共同的社会存在的肮脏规定，以及由科技精英创建并为科技精英创建的乌托邦“网络国家”的曙光。不那么宏大地说，这也是科技寡头们不缴纳公平份额税款的理由，以及他们试图通过学券、私有化和监管俘获从公共部门榨取资源。在某种程度上，我们的科技巨头意识到他们的财富是建立在他人的基础上的，就像其他通过从公地榨取财富而积累财富的大亨一样，他们担心如果工人设法超越他们既定的社会阶层或否则会变得更难以控制，因为他们用自己的大脑组织起来反对他们的所有者和管理者。你可以在纯粹的学术学科和职业教育之间的紧张关系中追溯这种恐惧的现代历史，这种恐惧随着现代商学院的出现而出现在美国舞台上。商业教育将有抱负的管理者的培训推崇为圣，以征服工业时代资本主义的堡垒，而对智力发展只不过是口头上的承诺。即使在这一章程宣扬了对知识的积极工具化追求，早期的商学院也对任何可能导致工人评估管理阶层能力的指导保持警惕。正如霍夫施塔特所写：“当哈佛商学院院长华莱士·唐纳姆向中西部的一所此类学校建议开设一门有关工会主义问题的课程时，他被告知：我们不希望我们的学生关注任何可能引起他们头脑中管理或商业政策问题的事情。”同样的自我造成的近视课程是通过科技寡头的大胆声明来实现的，因为他们预测一种基于知识传播的无摩擦社会秩序，旨在促进知识传播他们自己的阶级利益。毕竟，硅谷的大部分财富都是建立在其他人的智力成果之上的，这些成果通常是在大学中产生并由政府资助的。他们被誉为社会进步先锋的 STEM 学科不仅植根于科学，也植根于人文学科。然而，由于对知识的无拘无束的追求对他们来说是一种厌恶，所以他们从不承认这种特殊的智力债务。相反，他们聘请语言学家来改进其新兴人工智能帝国的大型语言模型，同时贬低那些成为语言学家的人。他们还喜欢 JD Vance 式的工人阶级 LARPing。再次追随 MAGA 运动的虚假民粹主义领导，科技寡头们将颂扬惠特曼式的美国被遗忘工人的美德，当然不会送自己的孩子去焊接学校或鼓励他们成为暖通空调技术员。当然，领导着美国企业中最不屈不挠的劳工卡特尔之一的硅谷寡头们，都恶毒地反对商人成立工会。作为 IBEW 当地巡线工的女儿，她已经 60 多岁了，还在爬电线杆，兼职做承包工作，我在这里看到了一种居高临下的态度，特别是当这些风险投资家嘴上说“值得尊敬的工作”时。这是受人尊敬的工作，但在一定年龄也是一种体力消耗大、破坏性大的工作，而且有最高收入上限。没有工会组织，在一个社会安全网薄弱的国家，这些行业的工作几乎无法提供任何保障或保护——同样的寡头们很乐意将其完全摧毁。但这些寡头比工人更需要工人，他们也知道这一点，尽管安德森最近声明“没有我们（科技寡头），除了停滞之外什么都没有。”这种对行业及其对工薪阶层男性的价值的强调也与科技领域的另一种万斯式压力有关：寡头们反动地坚持认为，性别等级制度只是精英政治而不是父权制的函数。现在女性获得的硕士学位比男性多，随之而来的必然是研究生教育毫无用处。这种反动性别意识形态的一个明显推论是科技兄弟们对体力的普遍痴迷——他们幼稚地认为体力是女性无法复制或超越的力量，并将其视为衡量自己与其他男性相比的一个载体。这也不是什么新鲜事。在总结 19 世纪对精神生活的看法时，霍夫施塔特写道：“人们认为学校教育的存在不是为了培养某些独特的精神品质，而是为了使个人进步成为可能。为此，立即参与生活中的实际任务被认为更具教育意义，而智力和文化追求则被认为是超凡脱俗的、非男性化的和不切实际的。”科技兄弟对进步的崇拜不仅仅只是为了保护护城河。他们围绕自己阶级的成员资格建立起来。针对自我进步以外的目标的知识是一种威胁，原因很简单，知情的民众是公民积极的民众。你不能在一群科技专家面前宣扬自动尊重一群受过教育、了解自己作为社会进步推动者角色的工人。这是硅谷知识精英不诚实姿态的另一个讽刺。那些喜欢吹嘘自己高智商、哀叹社会缺乏批判性思维、抱怨其他人都过于情绪化的人，却暴露出他们在面对自己的认知结构时的惊人失败。人类与动物的区别在于我们有能力思考自己的存在并将复杂的知识代代相传。这种让物种永存的努力植根于复杂的神经过程，这些过程涉及这些人在教条和无知的蔑视中所持有的智力。毕竟，情感是一种进化适应，会促进亲社会行为，而不仅仅是女性所拥有的愚蠢的可有可无的品质。 （在科技兄弟会自诩为纯逻辑使徒的人中，这一点也得到了广泛的展示，任何对他们充满不满的社交媒体账户的粗略查询都会很容易地证实这一点。）但在其首选的公共话语模式中，科技精英聚集在他们的首席（也是字面上的）书呆子马克·安德森（Marc Andreessen）的无知陈词背后，他公开吹嘘自己积极避免利用任何这些不同形式的元认知来思考任何事情。所有。这大概清空了他大脑中所有令人不安的思考，除了马克·安德森的伟大这一中心组织主题，以及构成马克·安德森的遗产和银行账户的未来的任何东西。我们需要理智主义，因为我们需要自由民主。这正是为什么这些家伙——他们都是家伙——不喜欢它。硅谷孕育的反智主义的典型代表是自封的新反动博主柯蒂斯·亚文（Curtis Yarvin），他是安德森、万斯和蒂尔的最爱。亚文公开拥护种族主义伪科学，并宣扬科技大亨的独裁统治愿景，在他的讲述中，这种统治将把加州变成一种封建君主制，首席执行官斜杠国王以仁慈的独裁者的身份管理一切。由于科技寡头们有一个最喜欢的思想家可以将他们的思想外包给他，所以就是他。雅文是这样评价他的模范国家元首的：“我们可以用财务术语来定义责任。如果我们将加州视为一家盈利的公司，一种资本资产，其目的是最大化现金生产，那么我们对责任的定义不仅是精确和明确的，而且是定量的。”按照这种观点，国家及其政府存在的理由应该是盈利，而科技首席执行官应该控制这一切。讨厌的人民只被授予作为对他们的领主心存感激的附庸的角色。否则，他们为理解自己的生活而做出的任何努力都会颠覆雅文的国王幻想。独裁，但要让它商业化。 不难理解为什么安德森和他的亲信认为那个说科技首席执行官应该成为加州独裁者的人是一个天才。但黑色幽默的是，在个人层面上，他们都认为这种情况下的独裁者显然是像他们自己一样的人，而不是另一种财富囤积者可能会发现自己的存在及其垄断帝国构成威胁。入门级的单一政治理论或哲学课程将迫使他们花五分钟思考这种情况及其历史先例的利弊。但是，当有播客可以继续播放、有表情包可以发推文时，你不可能指望忙碌的硅谷领主们花时间阅读很长的书籍并检查复杂的细微差别和矛盾。毕竟，时间就是金钱。在雅文看来，民主的邪恶力量代表着他称之为“大教堂”的麻木共识集团——教育机构、记者、文化创造者。这大概包括我，一位住在布鲁克林的中产阶级作家，她相信自由民主，并将她的孩子送到公立学校。亚文会辩称，我正在给你洗脑，让你拒绝像他的“首席执行官独裁者”这样的想法，这种想法不能因其本身的优点而被驳回，而只能通过阴谋来驳回。最终，这就是它的核心：科技世界的反知识分子亚文主义者重视秩序而不是变化——具体来说，是一种他们掌控的秩序，而不必担心诸如人口结构变化、竞争或任何错误等恼人的事情。他们口头上支持创新，但讨厌产生新奇和原创思想的深层脑力劳动和创造力。只有当这些东西能变成每月 20 美元的订阅服务，然后被利用成关键任务的企业软件时，他们才会关心这些东西。这种精神食利模式将使他们变得更加富有，这将继续支撑他们对 Techcrunch 峰会和 TED 演讲的无休止的倒退，在那里他们可以做他们唯一关心的有形工作：互相比拼，就像幼儿园的孩子在操场上吹嘘谁拥有最好的玩具一样。他们不想思考，当他们交换想法时，他们会重复使用已经在上层阶级成员中赢得惰性效忠的相同想法。如果他们不知何故陷入了一种陌生的（因此是原创的）思想结构，那么它很可能会屈服于精英八卦的堕落，而他们已经设法将其提升为自鸣得意的大亨的无所不知的话语。温和地说，这是一种可怕的事态，因为这些人拥有太多的权力，而他们对自己可以做什么的限制却太少。他们重视自己的专业知识，但本能地嘲笑他人的专业知识，尤其是那些鲁莽地要求在公共生活中发表意见并要求在没有财富作为每项社会福利的仲裁者的情况下对我们的社会如何构建有发言权的人。但至少现在，他们一直在亮出自己的手——对于那些想要智胜他们的人来说，这是一个有用的弱点。一如既往，感谢您的阅读！如果您愿意，这里有一些支持我的工作的方法。这里有一个随机的东西，感觉有点像老式互联网，供您欣赏。伊丽莎白

Original Summary

  1. I recently re-read Richard Hofstadter's Anti-Intellectualism in American Life, and now every time a reactionary Silicon Valley billionaire opens his mouth, I think about it. So I wrote about it for The Nation. Here's the column: On Instagram, there’s an activist named Brian Patrick (@pano.dime) who has dedicated his account to “posting an insane thing an AI executive said every day in 2026.” I can’t stop thinking about his entry for Day 15, quoting the CEO of a company called Suno, Mikey Shulman, as he claimed that musicians hate the process of making music. “It’s not really enjoyable to make music now,” he said. “It takes a lot of time, a lot of practice, you need to get really good at an instrument or really good at a piece of production software. I think a majority of people don’t enjoy the majority of the time they spend making music.”This would be news to every professional musician I know, and I live in a part of Brooklyn that’s adjacent to a neighborhood I think of as Dad Band Land because it’s populated by a disproportionate number of aging indie rockers with kids. But it’s not the ludicrousness of Shulman’s statement that sticks with me; it’s the swaggering know-nothing elan behind it, which is symptomatic of Silicon Valley’s deep-seated anti-intellectualism.As the historian Richard Hofstadter noted, a fierce anti-intellectual spirit has long animated American culture, but it has typically targeted the knowledge elite from below. What’s striking about today’s brand of anti-intellectualism is that it infuses the American knowledge elite; it stems from the bedrock conviction among tech oligarchs that they have mastered everything and have nothing left to learn. In this cloistered vision of tech-driven learning, they believe that deep intellectual work—the kind you do when you author a complex piece of music, for example—has little or no inherent value. Their disdain for it has fueled their attacks on higher education, the humanities, and learning for its own sake, which they believe has no purpose beyond its inevitable digitization and monetization.The examples are everywhere: Peter Thiel’s crusade against college attendance and his program that subsidizes high school students who want to forgo it, Marc Andreessen’s boasts that he actively avoids introspection, the gleeful prediction of Thiel’s Palantir colleague Alex Karp that AI will hurt educated women the most. That all of these scourges of learning for learning’s sake are themselves beneficiaries of privileged educations doesn’t matter: As ardent monopolists, they’ve managed to believe they’ve cornered the market on critical thinking. Everyone else needn’t be troubled by the rigors of learning, since they exist solely to serve as drones in the tech regimes of the future.The irony of this posture is that there’s almost no sector of American life—with the notable exception of the tech world’s political retainers in the Trump White House—that is less welcoming to rigorous thinking than Silicon Valley. The apostles of algorithmic dominance cheerlead chatbots and technocratic shortcuts for thinking and reasoning, and use them extensively themselves, even though the models hallucinate and have a baleful tendency toward sycophancy. “Researchers found that nearly a dozen leading models were highly sycophantic,” a recent New York Times story on the explosion in AI chatbots reported, “taking the users’ side in interpersonal conflicts 49 percent more often than humans did—even when the user described situations in which they broke the law, hurt someone or lied.” The obsequious intellectual concierges of the AI revolution also reduce cognitive strain on users, which further weakens their capacity for thinking. An MIT Media Lab study titled “Your Brain on ChatGPT” found that LLM users “consistently underperformed at neural, linguistic, and behavioral levels.” The tech oligarchs have somehow managed to enshittify thinking.This shouldn’t come as any great surprise to students of the dismally incurious and claustral mindscape of Silicon Valley. Tech oligarchs have erected a new cognitive technology designed to fry users’ brains after they’ve effectively lobotomized themselves with a real-world version of the same process. Our tech lords have long made a practice of outsourcing their thinking to the many people (and technologies) devoted to digesting difficult material and summarizing it for them. In their working lives, they then proceed to surround themselves with yes men and peers who affirm everything they say; the beta version of the cringy displays of great-leader sycophancy that break out in every Trump cabinet meeting was perfected in the boardrooms of Silicon Valley.This lovingly tended bubble of privilege makes it easy for tech oligarchs to avoid any of the discomfort that comes with questioning their modes of existence or confronting even minor levels of adversity. A tweet from a few years ago neatly summarized the mental costs of this lifestyle: “Being a billionaire must be insane. You can buy new teeth, new skin. All your chairs cost 20,000 dollars and weigh 2,000 pounds. Your life is just a series of your own preferences. In terms of cognitive impairment, it’s probably like being kicked in the head by a horse every day.”The tech lords’ ethos of intellectual secession is also rooted in two key maladies of American society: a general disdain for the intellectual class; and the overclass’s wariness toward—and not infrequent open hostility to—upward class mobility, which still largely rests on access to higher education.Hofstadler’s 1964 Pulitzer Prize–winning book Anti-Intellectualism in American Life has aged in certain ways, but it brilliantly traces the dogmas of anti-intellectualism to our founding mythologies—most especially, to the veneration of the self-made man by the business class. The self-made man was always a self-serving fable meant to conceal the deep fissures of rule by a business aristocracy. Now that much of America’s wealth is inherited or the product of luck and equity appreciation that is wildly disproportionate to the material contributions of any founder or CEO, our billionaire entrepreneurs and business owners are even less self-made than they used to be.Still, the myth persists, and you can see it in the tech oligarchy’s insistence that they owe the rest of society nothing as a consequence of their own Promethean genius. That’s the logic behind Silicon Valley’s vision of complete oligarch defection from the grubby dictates of social existence in common with fallen humanity and the dawn of a utopian “networked state” created by and for the tech elite. Less grandiosely, it’s also the tech oligarchs’ rationale for not paying their fair share of taxes, and their attempts to extract resources from the public sector via school vouchers, privatization, and regulatory capture.On some level, our tech lords are aware that their wealth is built on the backs of others, and like other moguls who’ve built fortunes by extracting wealth from the commons, they fear what would happen if workers manage to transcend their preordained social class or otherwise become more difficult to control because they’ve used their brains to organize against their owners and managers.You can trace the modern history of this fear in the tension between purely academic disciplines and vocational education, which arrived on the American scene alongside the advent of the modern business school. Business education canonized the training of aspiring managers to commandeer the redoubts of industrial-age capitalism and paid little more than lip service to intellectual development.Even under this charter solemnizing an aggressively instrumentalized pursuit of knowledge, early business schools were wary of any instruction that might cause workers to evaluate the competency of the managerial class. As Hofstadter writes: “When Dean Wallace Donham of the Harvard Graduate School of Business suggested to one such school in the Middle West that it offer a course on the problems of trade unionism, he was told: We don’t want our students to pay attention to anything that might raise questions about management or business policy in their minds.”The same self-inflicted myopia courses through the bold pronouncements of the tech oligarchs as they forecast a frictionless social order operating on the diffusion of knowledge designed to promote their own class interests. After all, much of Silicon Valley’s wealth is built on the intellectual work of others, often produced in universities and funded by the government. The STEM disciplines they hail as the vanguard of social progress are rooted not just in the sciences but the humanities as well. Yet since the unfettered quest for knowledge is anathema to them, they never acknowledge this particular intellectual debt. Instead, they hire linguists to improve the large language models of their burgeoning AI empires while disparaging the kind of people who become linguists.They also enjoy a bit of JD Vance–style working-class LARPing on the side. Again following the faux-populist lead of the MAGA movement, tech oligarchs will wax Whitmanian on the virtues of America’s forgotten workers without of course ever sending their own children to welding school or encouraging them to become HVAC technicians. And as a matter of course, the oligarchs of Silicon Valley, who have presided over one of the most unyielding labor cartels in American enterprise, all viciously oppose unionization for tradespeople.As the daughter of an IBEW local lineman who was still climbing power poles well into his 60s and doing contracting work on the side, I recognize a telltale attitude of patronizing condescension here—particularly when these venture capitalists mouth the words “respectable work.” It is respectable work, but it’s also work that is physically exhausting and destructive at a certain age, and has a ceiling for maximum income. Absent union organization, work in these trades offers little security or protection in a country with a weak social safety net—one that the same oligarchs would happily destroy altogether. But these oligarchs need workers more than workers need them, and they know it, despite Andreessen’s recent statement that “without us [tech oligarchs] there’s nothing but stagnation.”This emphasis on trades and their value to working class men in particular is also of a piece with another Vance-ian strain within the tech set: the oligarchs’ reactionary insistence that gender hierarchies are simply a function of meritocracy and not patriarchy. Now that women are getting more master’s degrees than men, it has to follow that graduate education is useless.A clear corollary of this reactionary gender ideology is the tech bros’ widespread obsession with physical strength—they view it, childishly, as a power that women cannot replicate or exceed, and treat it as a vector for measuring themselves against other men. This is not new either. In summarizing the 19th-century view toward the life of the mind, Hofstadter writes that “it was assumed that schooling existed not to cultivate certain distinctive qualities of mind but to make personal advancement possible. For this purpose, an immediate engagement with the practical tasks of life was held to be more usefully educative, whereas intellectual and cultural pursuits were called unworldly, unmasculine, and impractical.”The tech bros’ cult of advancement serves to do much more than safeguarding the moat they’ve erected around membership in their own class. Knowledge directed toward goals other than self-advancement is a threat, for the simple reason that an informed populace is a civically active populace. You can’t preach automatic deference before a caste of tech savants to a group of workers schooled in understanding their own role as agents of social progress.This is the other irony of the disingenuous posturing of Silicon Valley’s knowledge elite. The same people who like to tout their own high IQs, bemoan the lack of critical thinking in society, and complain that everyone else is too emotional betray an astounding failure to confront their own cognitive makeup. What separates humans from animals is our ability to contemplate our own existence and transfer complex knowledge down through generations. This species-perpetuating endeavor is rooted in complex neurological processes that involve the kind of intellectual capacities that these guys hold in dogmatic and ill-informed contempt.Emotion, after all, is an evolutionary adaptation that feeds into pro-social behavior, not a just silly dispensable quality women have. (It’s also on lavish display among the self-styled logic-only apostles of the tech brotherhood, as any cursory consultation of their grievance-addled social-media accounts will readily confirm.) But in its preferred modes of public discourse, the tech elite rallies behind the clueless bromides of their chief (and literal) egghead, Marc Andreessen, who openly brags that he actively avoids utilizing any of these various forms of meta cognition to contemplate anything at all. This presumably empties his brain of all troubling reflections beyond the central organizing theme of the greatness of Marc Andreessen, and whatever constitutes the future of Marc Andreessen’s legacy and bank accounts.We need intellectualism because we need liberal democracy. And that is precisely why these guys—they’re all guys—don’t like it. The poster boy for Valley-bred anti-intellectualism is the self-styled neoreactionary blogger Curtis Yarvin, an Andreessen, Vance, and Thiel favorite. Yarvin openly embraces racist psuedo-science and promotes a tech baron’s vision of autocratic rule that in his telling would transform California into a kind of feudal monarchy where a CEO-slash-king runs everything as a benevolent authoritarian. Inasmuch as tech oligarchs have a favored thinker to outsource their thinking to, he’s it. Here’s how Yarvin would evaluate his model head of state: “We can define responsibility in financial terms. If we think of California as a profitable corporation, a capital asset whose purpose is to maximize the production of cash, we have a definition of responsibility which is not only precise and unambiguous, but indeed quantitative.” In this view, the raison d’être of the state and its government should be profit making, and a tech CEO should control all of it. The pesky Volk are granted roles only as grateful vassals of their overlords; otherwise, any effort on their part to understand their own lives as meaningful would upend Yarvin’s kingly reveries. Dictatorship, but make it business.It’s not too hard to understand why Andreessen and his cronies think the guy who says a tech CEO should be the dictator of California is a genius. But it is darkly funny that, at the individual level, they all assume the authoritarian in this scenario would obviously be someone like themselves—not another wealth hoarder who might find their existence and their monopolist empires a threat. A single political theory or philosophy class at the intro level would force them to spend five minutes thinking about the pros and cons of this scenario and its historical precedents. But you can’t possibly expect the harried lords of Silicon Valley to spend time reading very long books and examining complex nuances and contradictions when there are podcasts to go on and memes to tweet. Time is money, after all.In Yarvin’s view, the sinister forces of democracy are represented in a numbing bloc of consensus he calls “the Cathedral”—educational institutions, journalists, culture makers. This presumably includes me, a middle-class writer living in Brooklyn who believes in liberal democracy and sends her kid to public school. Yarvin would argue I am brainwashing you into rejecting things like his “chief executive dictator” idea, which cannot be dismissed on its own merits, but only via conspiracy.Ultimately, this is the core of it: The anti-intellectual Yarvinites of the tech world value order over change—specifically, an order where they are in control and do not have to worry about nettlesome things like changing demographics, competition, or being wrong about anything at all. They pay lip service to innovation but hate the deep mental work and creativity that produces novelty and original thought. They care about such things only if they can be turned into a $20-a-month subscription service and then parlayed into mission-critical enterprise software.This model of mental rentiership will make them still more galactically rich, which will continue to underwrite their endless regress of Techcrunch summits and TED talks where they can do the only tangible work they care about: one-upping each other like kindergarteners on a playground bragging about who has the best toys. They do not want to think, and when they exchange ideas, they recycle the same ones that have already won inert allegiance among their fellow members of the overclass. If they were somehow to stumble into an unfamiliar (and therefore original) thought formation, it would in all likelihood succumb to the degraded rounds of elite gossip that they’ve managed to elevate into the omniscient discourse of self-congratulatory moguldom.This is, to put it mildly, a terrible state of affairs because these people have far too much power and they countenance far too few constraints on what they can do with it. They value their own expertise, but reflexively deride that of others—especially anyone who has the temerity to demand a voice in public life and a say in how our society is constructed without wealth as the arbiter of every social good. But for now, at least, they keep showing their hand—a useful weakness to exploit for those who wish to outsmart them.As always, thank you for reading! Here are some ways to support my work, if you're inclined. And here is a random thing that feels kind of old school Internet, for your enjoyment. Elizabeth

评论（默认中文）

tevon 2026/04/02 06:59
这似乎错过了很多很多点的情节。如果您先来到评论部分，则不值得一读。

当其提出的一个关键点是“技术是建立在他人的支持之上”时，甚至不讨论开源。

This seems to miss the plot on so, so many points. Not worth a read if you came to the comment section first.

Doesn't even discuss open-source when a key point its making is "tech is built on the backs of others".

在 HN 中查看
therobots927 2026/04/02 09:25
这里的科技可以指很多东西。鉴于文章中所述的上下文（以及字面上的标题），我认为很明显作者正在谈论 SV 精英 根据定义，它排除了大多数开源社区。不，一家科技公司发布开源代码在他们印制的大量现金面前并没有多大意义。但你应该把头埋进沙子里，这样会感觉好一些。
Tech here can refer to many things. Given the context stated in the article (and literally the title) I think it’s clear the author is talking about SV elites which by definition excludes most of the open source community. And no, a tech company releasing open source code doesn’t amount to much in the face of the firehouse of cash they print. But you should just stick your head back in the sand, it’ll feel better.
在 HN 中查看
selfunaware9 2026/04/02 05:28
发表这篇模因文章的好日子
Good choice of a day to publish this meme article
在 HN 中查看
nativeit 2026/04/02 08:33
生成这个模因帐户的日子是一个不错的选择。
Good choice of a day to spawn this meme account.
在 HN 中查看
therobots927 2026/04/02 09:30
我一直这么说，你可以检查我的评论历史来验证 - HN 被恶意破坏到了极端程度。建立额外的账户绝对没有任何限制，任何称职的黑客都可以轻松地建立一个法学硕士来对相关的想法提出明显的反对。很明显 HN 对此绝对没有问题。

不可原谅的是，社区中的很大一部分人看到了文章背后的逻辑，但却避免参与其中，因为担心激怒某些未来的雇主，或者只是因为他们想避免在这里发生对抗。我们正在实时目睹科技亿万富翁迎来一个反乌托邦社会。我们想具体讨论什么？显然，除了彼得·蒂尔（Peter Thiel）在房间里塑造的大象之外，什么都没有。

I keep saying this and you can check my comment history to verify - HN is botted to an extreme degree. There’s absolutely no restrictions on spinning up additional accounts and any hacker worth their salt could easily spin up an LLM to set up apparent opposition to a linked idea. It’s clear HN has absolutely no problem with this.

What is inexcusable is the large fraction of the community that sees the logic behind the article but avoids getting involved for fear of irritating some future employer, or just because they want to avoid confrontation here. We’re watching tech billionaires usher in a dystopian society in real time. And we want to talk about what exactly? Apparently anything but the Peter Thiel shaped elephant in the room.

在 HN 中查看
nsingh2 2026/04/02 09:59
考虑到我在每篇帖子中看到了多少[死]评论（您可以通过在用户个人资料中设置 `showdead` 来启用），似乎 HN 正在采取一些措施来解决这个问题。

我最近才启用它，所以我不知道LLM时代之前死评论有多频繁。

Seems like HN is doing something to combat this, considering how many [dead] comments I see in every post (which you can enable by setting `showdead` in your user profile).

I've only recently enabled it so I don't know how frequent dead comments were before the LLM era.

在 HN 中查看
therobots927 2026/04/02 10:08
很公平。事实上，我在发表这条评论后就注意到了这一点。
Fair enough. I actually noticed that right after I posted this comment.
在 HN 中查看
trinsic2 2026/04/02 06:14
我查阅了反智主义，这是定义：

>反智主义是对科学、高等教育和批判性思想的深刻怀疑或敌视，通常将知识分子视为超然的精英 。在民粹主义政治、宗教教条和经济焦虑的驱动下，它表现为对证据和科学共识的拒绝。它通过优先考虑情感叙述而不是专家分析来破坏民主决策

我想说的是，还有另一种可能性。专家和专家意见很容易受到社交媒体回声室的相同问题的影响[0]。

新的想法和想法往往会被拒绝，因为专家往往过于依赖在航母过程中建立的立场。

因此，反智主义的概念不仅仅基于情感反应。但也是基于这种对并不总是存在的情况创造太多绝对确定性的概念。由于这种回音室，人们倾向于拒绝某些信息的科学依据，因为这种困境往往会忽视其他不为人所知的因素。科学追求也有可能被不良行为者所利用。

[0]: https://truenorthoutreach.com/the-science-of-echo-chambers-h...

I looked up anti-intellectualism and here is the definition:

>Anti-intellectualism is a profound skepticism or hostility toward science, higher education, and critical thought, often viewing intellectuals as detached elites . Driven by populist politics, religious dogma, and economic anxiety, it manifests as rejection of evidence and scientific consensus. It undermines democratic decision-making by prioritizing emotional narratives over expert analysis

I would say that there is another possibility to this. Experts and Expert opinions are susceptible to the same problem of social media echo-chambers[0].

Where new ideas and thought tend to be rejected because experts tend to rely too strongly in positions established over the course of a carrier.

So the concept of anti-intellectualism is not solely based on emotional responses. But also based on this concept of creating too much absolute certainty about a situation that doesn't always exist. People have a tendency to reject scientific basis of some information because of this echo-chamber as this dilemma tends to ignore other factors that are not well known. Also scientific pursuits have the possibility of being game by bad actors.

[0]: https://truenorthoutreach.com/the-science-of-echo-chambers-h...

在 HN 中查看
voxl 2026/04/02 07:58
科学与你决定为这条线索增色的随机淋浴思想之间的区别在于，科学有一些健全的认识论基础，通常是证据，而你却什么都没有。
The difference between science and this random shower thought you decided to grace this thread with is that science has some sound epistemological basis, typically evidence, whereas you have nothing.
在 HN 中查看
trinsic2 2026/04/02 08:13
> 科学与你决定为这条线索增色的随机淋浴思想之间的区别在于，科学有一些健全的认识论基础

科学可以有健全的认识论基础，但很多时候，过度专业化会迫使人们对没有认识论基础的领域产生信心，并通过该领域的人们的回声来保持对某一主题的权威感。

> The difference between science and this random shower thought you decided to grace this thread with is that science has some sound epistemological basis

Science can have sound epistemological basis, but many times overspecialization can force confidence in areas where there is none and its echo-chambered by people in the field to keep the sense of authority on a subject going.

Viliam1234 2026/04/02 08:55
黑客新闻精英的反智主义。

无论你喜欢与否，人工智能都是编程中有用的工具。是的，有很多炒作。也有很多无知。 AI 不会为您编写整个复杂的应用程序，但可以轻松地使其开发速度提高 10 倍。

The Anti-Intellectualism of the Hacker News Elites.

AIs are useful tools in programming, whether you like it or not. Yes, there is a lot of hype. There is also a lot of ignorance. AI is not going to write an entire complex application for you, but can easily make its development 10x faster.
